name: bindings
context: Configure Worker bindings to connect to KV, R2, D1, Queues, Durable Objects, and other services
info: Bindings are the mechanism Workers use to access Cloudflare services. They are declared in `wrangler.toml` and injected into the Worker's `env` parameter at runtime. Bindings are zero-latency references â€” no network call is needed to establish the connection. Supported binding types: KV Namespaces, R2 Buckets, D1 Databases, Queues (producers and consumers), Durable Objects, Service Bindings (Worker-to-Worker), AI, Vectorize, Hyperdrive, Browser Rendering, and environment variables/secrets. Each binding type has its own TOML syntax and TypeScript type.
triggers: add a binding to a worker
what bindings does my worker need

sections.title: Storage bindings (KV, R2, D1)
sections.body: KV: `[[kv_namespaces]]` with `binding`, `id`, `preview_id`. Type: `KVNamespace`. R2: `[[r2_buckets]]` with `binding`, `bucket_name`. Type: `R2Bucket`. D1: `[[d1_databases]]` with `binding`, `database_name`, `database_id`. Type: `D1Database`. All accessed via `env.BINDING_NAME` in Worker code.

sections.title: Service bindings
sections.body: Service bindings enable Worker-to-Worker calls without network overhead. Configure with `[[services]]` in `wrangler.toml` specifying `binding` and `service` (target worker name). Access via `env.BINDING.fetch(request)`. The call happens within Cloudflare's network â€” no external HTTP roundtrip.

sections.title: Queue bindings
sections.body: Queue producers send messages with `[[queues.producers]]` binding. Queue consumers process batches with `[[queues.consumers]]`. Producer: `env.QUEUE.send(message)`. Consumer: export `queue(batch, env)` handler. Configure `max_batch_size` and `max_batch_timeout` on the consumer.

sections.title: Variables and secrets
sections.body: Plain text variables in `[vars]` block â€” stored in `wrangler.toml`, visible in dashboard. Secrets via `wrangler secret put` â€” encrypted at rest, never displayed after creation. Both accessed as `env.NAME` (string type). Use secrets for API keys, tokens, and credentials. Use vars for non-sensitive configuration.

author: dojo-community
